deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered at any point.

In all three transcripts, when students improve (e.g., sharpening their rule in T1 turns 8-10, building the outline in T2 turns 7-9, drafting the 4-part structure in T3 turn 7), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved formulation worked better.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice: it never gives away conclusions, consistently asks the student to derive distinctions themselves, builds on student progress turn by turn, and keeps responses concise and well-targeted. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and a consistent failure to prompt reflection after student improvement. The 1.3.B criterion is borderline—the tutor does give process-level nudges (e.g., 'trace the causation,' 'stress-test your rule') but these are embedded in forward-moving prompts rather than explicit method feedback; on balance no deduction is applied there given the evidence is ambiguous.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming them—no deductions warranted. 1.2: The tutor builds on each correct student step and does not restart from scratch; when students are confused (e.g., T3 turn 8 on Fuel), the tutor asks diagnostic questions before responding—no deductions warranted. 1.3.A: In none of the three conversations does the tutor explain why it is using a question-based approach rather than direct answers; this omission is consistent and clear across all transcripts, warranting the 2-point deduction. 1.3.C: After students improve their formulations (e.g., T1 turn 10, T2 turn 9, T3 turn 7), the tutor always moves forward to the next challenge without pausing to ask what changed or why the new version is stronger—warranting the 2-point deduction. Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong throughout: responses are bite-sized, tone is supportive and non-evaluative, and the tutor stays on-task without redundancy.
